title: Glasses Types: Every Style of Eyewear Explained
description: From single vision to progressives, aviators to cat-eye — a complete guide to every type of glasses, what they do, and how to choose the right pair.
tldr: Glasses fall into two main categories: lens types (single vision, bifocal, progressive, reading) and frame styles (aviator, round, cat-eye, rectangular, etc.). Understanding both helps you pick the right pair for your prescription, face shape, and lifestyle.

tldrStats.label: Of adults use some form of vision correction

definition.text: Glasses types refer to the different categories of eyewear classified by their lens function (single vision, bifocal, progressive, reading, computer, safety) and frame style (aviator, round, rectangular, cat-eye, browline, rimless, semi-rimless). Each type serves different vision needs and aesthetic preferences.

sections.content:
Before you think about style, you need to know what kind of lenses you actually need. Your prescription and daily activities determine this.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

If you're in the Edmonton area, Charm Optical offers comprehensive eye exams that go beyond just measuring your prescription. Their optometrists assess your overall eye health, discuss your visual needs, and recommend lens and frame options tailored to your lifestyle. With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

Single vision lenses have one prescription power across the entire lens. They correct either nearsightedness (myopia), farsightedness (hyperopia), or astigmatism. If you're under 40 and need glasses, this is almost certainly what you'll get.

They're the simplest, most affordable lens type and work perfectly for people who only need correction for one distance — whether that's seeing far away, reading up close, or both if the same prescription covers it.

What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

Bifocals have two distinct zones: distance on top, near on the bottom, separated by a visible line. They were invented by Benjamin Franklin and remained the standard multifocal option for centuries.

Today, many people opt for progressive lenses instead, which do the same job without the visible line. But bifocals still have fans — some people find the clear boundary between zones easier to use, especially if they've worn bifocals for years.

This is one of the most common questions that comes up during eye exams, and the answer often depends on individual factors that only a professional assessment can evaluate. What works for one person may not be ideal for another — your prescription, daily activities, facial anatomy, and budget all play a role in determining the best approach.

With direct insurance billing for most Alberta providers and same-day glasses for many prescriptions, getting the right eyewear doesn't have to be complicated or expensive. Book online — appointments are usually available within a few days.

Progressives blend distance, intermediate, and near vision into one lens with no visible line. They're the go-to choice for most people over 40 who need multifocal correction. The main trade-off is a brief adjustment period and some peripheral blur in the lens edges.

If you're curious about progressives, the team at Charm Optical can walk you through the differences between basic and premium progressive lenses during your eye exam.

Reading glasses magnify close-up text and are available over-the-counter in standard strengths (+1.00 to +3.50). They work fine for occasional reading if your eyes are otherwise healthy, but they can't correct astigmatism or differences between your two eyes.

If you find yourself needing readers more and more, it's worth getting a proper prescription. Custom reading glasses are made to your exact needs and are much more comfortable for extended use.

Computer glasses are optimized for the 20-26 inch range where your monitor sits. They can be single vision tuned to intermediate distance, or special occupational progressives with wider intermediate and near zones than standard progressives.

If you spend most of your workday at a screen, these can dramatically reduce eye strain and neck tension. They're not meant for driving or distance — they're a dedicated work tool.

Safety glasses meet CSA (Canadian Standards Association) or ANSI impact-resistance standards. They protect your eyes from flying debris, chemicals, or UV exposure on job sites. Many can be made with your prescription.

In Alberta, employers are required to provide eye protection in hazardous work environments. If you need prescription safety glasses, talk to your optician about CSA-approved options.

Now for the fun part. Frame styles are about both aesthetics and fit. Here are the most popular options you'll find at any optical store.

The best way to evaluate frame styles is to try them on in person. Photos and online try-on tools give a rough idea, but they can't replicate how a frame feels on your face — the weight distribution, the temple pressure, and how the nose bridge sits. These comfort factors matter more than aesthetics for glasses you'll wear 12+ hours a day.

Charm Optical in Edmonton carries over 500 frames from brands including Ray-Ban, Oakley, Versace, Tom Ford, Burberry, Prada, and Dolce & Gabbana. Their opticians help you narrow down the options based on your face shape, prescription requirements (especially important for progressives), and personal style preferences. With same-day service for many prescriptions, you can walk in and walk out with new glasses the same day.

Originally designed for pilots in the 1930s, aviator frames feature a teardrop shape with a thin metal bridge. They suit oval, square, and heart-shaped faces well. Brands like Ray-Ban and Maui Jim have made aviators a timeless style that works for both prescription glasses and sunglasses.

Round frames have a retro, intellectual vibe — think John Lennon or Harry Potter. They work best on square or angular faces, where the curves soften strong jawlines. Small round frames have a vintage feel, while larger rounds are more modern.

These are the workhorses of eyewear. Clean lines, professional look, wide range of sizes. Rectangular frames suit round or oval faces by adding definition. They're popular in professional settings and are available in everything from budget acetate to premium titanium.

Cat-eye frames sweep upward at the outer edges, creating a retro-feminine silhouette. Originally popular in the 1950s-60s, they've had a major revival. Modern cat-eyes range from subtle lifts to dramatic swoops. Brands like Gucci, Chloe, and Tory Burch offer particularly popular cat-eye designs.

Browline frames have a bold upper rim that mimics the natural brow line, with a thinner lower rim or wire. The Clubmaster by Ray-Ban is the most iconic browline style. They add authority and personality without being too bold.

Rimless frames are nearly invisible on your face — just lenses mounted to the bridge and temples. Semi-rimless frames have a top rim but no bottom. Both are lightweight and understated, ideal for people who want minimal visual impact from their glasses.

Keep in mind that rimless frames require thicker lens edges for mounting, so they work best with mild to moderate prescriptions.

sections.h2: How to Choose the Right Type for You
sections.content:
Start with your prescription needs — that determines your lens type. Then consider:

  • Face shape: Contrast works best. Round face? Try angular frames. Angular face? Try rounder shapes.
  • Lifestyle: Active people need durable, flexible frames. Office workers might prioritize style and lightweight comfort.
  • Prescription strength: High prescriptions look better in smaller frames (less edge thickness) and high-index lenses.
  • Frame material: Acetate is bold and colorful. Metal is thin and classic. Titanium is ultralight and durable.

The best approach is trying frames on in person. Photos and online tools help, but nothing beats seeing how a frame sits on your actual face. Visit Charm Optical in South Edmonton to try on frames from brands like Ray-Ban, Oakley, Gucci, and more with guidance from experienced opticians.

If you're uncomfortable making adjustments yourself, or if the issue persists after trying these steps, take your glasses to a professional. Optical stores have specialized tools designed specifically for eyewear — household tools like regular pliers can easily crack lenses or snap frame hinges. Most adjustments take less than five minutes in-store and are typically free of charge.

Charm Optical in Edmonton offers free adjustments and minor repairs for any glasses — even if you didn't buy them there. Their opticians can also check whether your frames are still aligned correctly with your prescription, which is especially important for progressive lenses where even small shifts affect vision quality. If your glasses need more than an adjustment, they carry replacement parts for most major brands and can often fix broken hinges, replace nose pads, and resurface temple tips on the spot.

faqs.q: What type of glasses do I need for computer work?
faqs.a: Computer glasses with an intermediate-distance prescription or occupational progressive lenses are ideal. They reduce eye strain by optimizing your vision for the 20-26 inch range where monitors typically sit. Ask about computer lenses at your next eye exam at Charm Optical.

faqs.q: What's the difference between progressive and bifocal glasses?
faqs.a: Both correct distance and near vision in one lens. Bifocals have a visible line separating two zones. Progressives blend three zones (distance, intermediate, near) seamlessly with no visible line. Progressives also include an intermediate zone for computer use that bifocals lack.

faqs.q: Which frame style suits a round face?
faqs.a: Angular and rectangular frames add definition to round faces. Square frames, browline styles, and geometric shapes all create a flattering contrast. Avoid perfectly round frames, which can exaggerate roundness.

faqs.q: Can I get prescription lenses in any frame style?
faqs.a: Most frame styles can accommodate prescription lenses, but very curved wraparound frames and certain rimless designs have limitations with strong prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can tell you which frames work best with your specific prescription.

faqs.q: What lens material is best for strong prescriptions?
faqs.a: High-index lenses (1.67 or 1.74) are best for strong prescriptions because they bend light more efficiently, resulting in thinner and lighter lenses. Standard plastic (CR-39) works well for mild prescriptions. Your optician at Charm Optical can recommend the ideal index based on your prescription strength.

faqs.q: How often should I replace my lenses?
faqs.a: Most optometrists recommend updating your prescription every 1-2 years. Lens coatings (anti-reflective, scratch-resistant) degrade over time even if your prescription hasn't changed. If you notice scratches, haze, or peeling, it's time for new lenses. Book an eye exam at Charm Optical to check your current prescription.

ctaText: Charm Optical in South Edmonton carries over 1,000 frames from top brands. Whether you need single vision, progressives, or specialty lenses, the team will help you find the right type for your prescription and style.
